coredata.dc:title: Energy, exergy and corrosion analysis of direct absorption solar collector employed with ultra-high stable carbon quantum dot nanofluid
coredata.dc:creator: Joseph, Albin
Thomas, Shijo
coredata.dc:description: Nanofluid offers remarkable thermal and optical properties favourable for direct solar absorption. The nanofluids prepared by the conventional two-step synthesis method have low colloidal stability, while that synthesized through the one-step method is costly. Hence the nanofluid synthesized using an economical one-step method has great significance. In the present study, a highly stable C-dot/water nanofluid was synthesized using an economical one-pot synthesis method. The optical characterisation, corrosion analysis and cost estimation of the nanofluid were conducted. The influence of C-dot/water nanofluid on the performance of direct absorption solar collector was analyzed. The direct absorption parabolic solar collector employed with C-dot/water nanofluid yielded a maximum thermal efficiency of 73.41% at Reynolds number of 2952, while that for water was 15.79%. Thermodynamic analysis of the system and cost estimation of the nanofluid was performed to establish its commercial suitability in various solar thermal devices. The maximum exergy destruction was found to be 924.3 W and was more or less constant at all flow rates. The main highlight of the new C-dot/water nanofluid is its significantly high colloidal stability and was found to be stable for more than six months. The corrosion rate of the new C-dot/water nanofluid was obtained as 0.094 mm/year, while that for the base fluid was 0.372 mm/year. With superior optical performance, corrosion resistance, and low production cost, the C-dot nanofluid has the potential to be a prospective working fluid in various direct absorption solar thermal systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-Dot nanofluid
Direct absorption
Colloidal stability
Corrosion analysis
Energy and exergy analysis
Parabolic collector

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimentation test rig for photo thermal conversion
Corrosion analysis of C-dot nanofluid on mild steel
Experimental performance evaluation of parabolic collector

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of C-dot nanofluid.
Schematic of the corrosion test apparatus.
a) Schematic of the direct absorption solar collector setup b) Actual parabolic collector system.
Schematic of the path of solar radiation in the direct absorption solar collector.
TEM images of synthesized C-dot.
EDAX spectrum of Cdot nanofluid.
FTIR spectrum of synthesized C-dot.
UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of C-dot nanofluid.
Transmittance spectrum of optimized C-Dot nanofluid.
Variation of penetration depth with absorptivity.
UV–vis–NIR Transmittance spectrum of C-dot nanofluid used before and after centrifugation.
Nyquist plot of C-dot nanofluid at various time interval and water.
Tafel polarization curve of C-dot nanofluid and water.
Schematic representation of mechanism behind the corrosion when exposed to water and nanofluid.
Variation of fluid outlet temperature with time at a) 60 L/h, b) 70 L/h, c) 80 L/h, d) 90 L/h.
Variation of thermal efficiency with time at a) 60 L/h, b) 70 L/h, c) 80 L/h, d) 90 L/h.
Variation of entropy generation with flow rate.
Variation of Exergy destruction with time at a) 60 L/h, b) 70 L/h, c) 80 L/h, d) 90 L/h.
Parameters of the collector.
Uncertainty values.
Viscosity of C-dot nanofluid.
Comparison of efficiency of nanofluid and water.
Comparison of experimental and theoretical data.
Comparison of the estimated cost of heat transfer fluids.

head.ce:title: Energy, exergy and corrosion analysis of direct absorption solar collector employed with ultra-high stable carbon quantum dot nanofluid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Calicut, 673601, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Calicut, 673601, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluid offers remarkable thermal and optical properties favourable for direct solar absorption. The nanofluids prepared by the conventional two-step synthesis method have low colloidal stability, while that synthesized through the one-step method is costly. Hence the nanofluid synthesized using an economical one-step method has great significance. In the present study, a highly stable C-dot/water nanofluid was synthesized using an economical one-pot synthesis method. The optical characterisation, corrosion analysis and cost estimation of the nanofluid were conducted. The influence of C-dot/water nanofluid on the performance of direct absorption solar collector was analyzed. The direct absorption parabolic solar collector employed with C-dot/water nanofluid yielded a maximum thermal efficiency of 73.41% at Reynolds number of 2952, while that for water was 15.79%. Thermodynamic analysis of the system and cost estimation of the nanofluid was performed to establish its commercial suitability in various solar thermal devices. The maximum exergy destruction was found to be 924.3 W and was more or less constant at all flow rates. The main highlight of the new C-dot/water nanofluid is its significantly high colloidal stability and was found to be stable for more than six months. The corrosion rate of the new C-dot/water nanofluid was obtained as 0.094 mm/year, while that for the base fluid was 0.372 mm/year. With superior optical performance, corrosion resistance, and low production cost, the C-dot nanofluid has the potential to be a prospective working fluid in various direct absorption solar thermal systems.

ce:section.ce:para: The increased demand for energy and subsequent depleting resources of fossil fuels has widened the gap between supply and demand. The lack of proper technologies in tackling the environmental pollution associated with these fuels is also another major challenge. These challenges motivated researchers and engineers worldwide to explore the potential of alternate sustainable and renewable energy sources like solar, wind, geothermal, tide, ocean waves, etc. Solar energy, being a potential renewable energy source, has gained wide attention due to its potential to be converted directly into electricity (photovoltaic conversion) and/or thermal energy (solar-thermal conversion). The feasibility of an optimistic future in solar thermal conversion have been witnessed in the past few years. Conventional solar thermal conversion systems work on the principle of surface absorption, where an opaque material absorbs and convert the solar energy into thermal energy. The thermal energy is then transferred from the absorber to the working fluid via conduction and convection. Investigations on surface absorption are focusing on enhancing the solar absorptivity of the absorber. A variety of coatings that include intrinsic absorbing coatings, fractal textured surface coatings, multilayer interference coating, Cermet composite coatings, etc., were used to achieve enhanced photothermal conversion and lower emissivity [1]. However, the prime motive of these researches was to improve the optical efficiency of the collector, whereas the thermal efficiency of the system was given less importance. Since the solar thermal conversion occurs on the absorber surface, the heat losses are found to be significant in surface absorption solar collectors. A transition in the technology of solar thermal conversion systems from the surface absorption or indirect absorption of solar irradiance to direct solar absorption is gaining prominence due to the higher efficiency of the latter [2].

The advent of nanotechnology and its smart approach to various engineering challenges have set aside many of the conventional systems. Nanofluids are envisaged as a potential working fluid to be used in solar thermal systems. The direct absorption or volumetric absorption solar collectors use optical nanofluids having high solar radiation absorbance, which also act as the working fluid to transfer the heat to the thermal systems (for power generation, heating, cooking, etc.) [3]. The avoidance of solar to thermal conversion on the absorber surface, as in the case of surface absorption, rules out the associated heat losses [4]. The optical absorptivity of nanofluids is of prime importance in deciding its suitability to be employed in direct absorption systems. The selection of base fluid is based on its operating temperature. The commonly used based fluids are water for low-temperature applications, ethylene glycol for temperatures up to 180 °C, and thermal oils for medium temperatures up to 350 °C [5]. The dispersion of nanoparticles in the base fluid considerably improves its solar absorptivity, which enhances its photothermal conversion, resulting in improved efficiency of the system. Tyagi et al. [6] reported that the absorptivity of the Al/water nanofluid was improved by nine times, as compared to the base fluid. Bhalla et al. [7] studied the direct solar radiation absorption capability of Al2O3/Co3O4 based aqueous nanofluid. The authors surmised that the optimized nanofluid shows 5.4 °C enhancement as compared to the water. The influence of carbon nanotube on direct absorption solar collector was investigated by Alibasksh et al. [8]. A maximum of 17% enhancement in collector efficiency was noted, as compared to the base fluid. Recently, researchers have utilised the plasmonic resonance properties of various noble nanomaterials for efficient photo-thermal conversion [9]. Sreekumar et al. [10] synthesized a novel ATO/Ag-based nanofluid and investigated its influence on direct absorption parabolic collector. They observed a maximum of 98.90% absorptivity for the optimized concentration, resulting in a collector efficiency of 63.5%. Investigation on thermal and optical properties of binary plasmonic CuO–SiO2/Ag nanofluid reveals that in the absence of CuO nanoparticles, the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid reduced drastically, whereas the SiO2/Ag considerably enhanced the photothermal conversion efficiency [11]. The potential of using various types of nanofluids like metal nanofluid [12], metal oxide nanofluid [13], plasmonic nanofluid [14], binary nanofluid [15], etc. on direct absorption systems have been established by many researchers. However, the colloidal stability of nanofluid is its main drawback. The agglomeration of particles and the gravitational effect on nanoparticles dispersed in the fluid results in sedimentation of the particles and adversely affect the homogeneity of the fluid properties. This limited the suitability of nanofluids for many applications, particularly those requiring a higher concentration of nanoparticles. Factors like size, surface charge, density, and concentration of the nanoparticles could affect the stability of the nanofluids [16]. The nanoparticles, once dispersed in the base fluid, experience many inter and intraparticle forces like drag force, lift force, Brownian forces, thermophoretic forces, etc. [17]. Among these, Brownian force is the most significant in the case of nanofluids used for heat transfer studies. Many researchers have reported it to be the main cause of thermal conductivity enhancement of nanofluids [18–20].

A number of studies are reported on the techniques to improve the colloidal stability of the nanofluid. The stability of nanofluids can be enhanced by electrostatic or steric stabilisation methods like tuning the pH of base fluid, the addition of surfactants, etc. [21,22]. But, varying the pH may disturb the electrochemical properties of the nanofluid and may result in a corrosive nanofluid [23,24]. Shifting the neutral pH of nanofluid to improve its colloidal stability would lead to accumulation of charges causing vigorous corrosion behaviour. This is due to the formation of various metal oxides on the surface of the substrates. Apart from the pH of the nanofluid, the surfactants used to stabilise the nanofluid could also influence its corrosion behaviour. Rashidi et al. [25] reported that the corrosion rate of carbon steel was unaffected in the presence of MWCNT/water-based nanofluid, while it exhibited a notable increase in corrosion resistance in the presence of surfactants like sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS).

Carbon-based nanofluids are extensively used in direct solar absorption systems due to their significant solar absorptivity. However, the colloidal stability of these nanofluids is less and is one of the practical drawbacks. Carbon allotropes like graphene, CNT, etc., exhibit comparatively larger sizes and hence affect colloidal stability. In addition, the cost associated with these materials is relatively high, which limits the practical feasibility of these nanofluids. Literature reveals that nanofluid synthesized through one step method exhibits better colloidal stability but are costly. In consideration of all these factors, a carbon-based material of smaller particle size synthesized through a low-cost one-step method has great potential in solar thermal conversion systems. Hence, in the present study, a low-cost synthesis of carbon nanofluid of particle size less than 10 nm, was done to obtain a highly stable nanofluid for solar thermal conversion. It is a known fact that the better stability of nanofluid is offered by smaller particles [16]. In addition, direct absorption solar collectors demand nanofluids of high concentration, which may lead to agglomeration of particles and reduced stability [9]. In spite of these, methods to enhance the stability of nanofluids employed in direct absorption collector is not much explored. Quantum dots, particles that exhibit a size less than 10 nm, is a potential candidate to develop stable nanofluids. Keblenski et al. [16] reported that quantum dot-based nanofluids offer higher colloidal stability. Furthermore, Brownian motion, the mechanism responsible for improved thermal conductivity, is predominant for lower sized particles.

Even though a number of nanofluids were analyzed to establish their influence on direct absorption solar collectors, colloidal stability, cost analysis, corrosion studies, etc., are not much explored. The present study aims to analyze all the parameters that influence the commercialisation of a nanofluid suited for solar parabolic collectors. Solar thermal conversion, colloidal stability, corrosion resistance, and cost estimation of carbon dots based nanofluid were investigated. Carbon dot nanofluid prepared using a one-step method was characterised, and its influence on direct absorption parabolic collector was analyzed. Carbon dot is an emerging nanostructure that exhibits a size less than 10 nm. Literature reveals that CQDs can be used for various applications like semiconductors, photocatalysis, drug delivery, etc. [23,24]. However, the properties of nanofluids using CQD are not much explored. There are only a limited number of articles analysing the heat transfer capabilities of CQD nanofluids. Carbon dot nanofluid was synthesized and optimized to achieve the enhanced photothermal conversion. The corrosion behaviour of the nanofluid was also studied to analyze its suitability in various solar thermal devices. Finally, a cost estimation was done to establish the suitability to commercialise the C-dot/Water nanofluid for solar collectors.

Ascorbic acid, C6H8O6, (Himedia), which is an eco-friendly and low-cost material, is the carbon source. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was employed to control the pH and copper acetate (Cu(AC)2.H2O) was used as the catalyst. All chemicals (except ascorbic acid) were procured from Merck and used without any further purification.

A simple one-step method was adopted to synthesize the C-dot nanofluid. 1625 mg of Ascorbic acid was dissolved in 1 L of DI water. The pH of DI water was varied from 7 to 14 using NaOH. Once the ascorbic acid is dissolved completely, 625 mg of copper acetate is added to the mixture and then stirred for 10 h at a temperature of 90 °C. The final resulted supernatant containing C-dot and DI water is collected using vacuum filtration. Since the final obtained c-dot is in supernatant form, the concentration of nanofluid was increased by vaporizing the excess water through boiling. The synthesis procedure involved is graphically presented in Fig. 1 .

Solar absorptivity of the nanofluid was estimated using the UV–vis–NIR spectroscopy. The cumulative amount of solar radiation absorbed (SWAF) by the nanofluid is estimated using the Eqn. (1), (2) and (3), and the values are presented in Table 1 .
(1)
SWAF =
∫
λ min λmax
I
( λ )
.
( 1 −
e
− E
( λ )
. l
)
dλ
∫
λ min λmax
I
( λ )
dλ
where, SWAF is the solar weighted absorption fraction and I(λ) is the spectral solar irradiance. Beer-Lambert Law [26] (Eqn. (2)) was used to estimate the extinction coefficient (E(λ)) of the nanofluid.
(2)
E
( λ )
= −
1
l
ln
( τ
( λ )
)
where, l and τ is the path length and transmittance of the nanofluid. The absorbed spectrum of the solar radiation was calculated using the Eqn. (3).
(3) IA(λ) = E(λ).IAM 1.5

Morphological analysis of the prepared C-dot nanofluid was conducted using Transmission electron microscopy. The optical properties were estimated using UV–vis–NIR spectroscopy (Shimadzu). The baseline of UV–vis–NIR spectrum was measured using air at room temperature. The transmittance spectrum of the nanofluid was analyzed to quantify the absorptivity of the fluids.

The centrifugation method was adopted to study the colloidal stability of nanofluid. The nanofluid was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The variation in the solar transmittance before and after the centrifugation was noted and compared. The centrifugation method of analysing stability is a qualitative technique for understanding the stability of the nanofluid.

The viscosity of the nanofluid is measured using the Rheometer (Brookfield ultra III). The viscosity was measured at various temperatures by employing a constant temperature attached to the rheometer. The viscosity was noted temperature ranging from 25 °C to 65 °C.

The corrosion behaviour of the optimized nanofluid was analyzed using the electrochemical workstation (Metrohm Autolab PGSTAT128N). A three-electrode cell was used to analyze the corrosion behaviour of optimized C-dot nanofluid. Fig. 2 present the schematic of the corrosion test apparatus using the three-electrode cell. In this experimentation, the nanofluid is exposed to mild steel with an area of 1.33 cm2, which is the working electrode. The nanofluid exposed to the working electrode act as the electrolyte, while graphite rod and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The corrosion behaviour of C-Dot nanofluid is then compared to that of base fluid (water). Since the water is neutral, the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte was developed by introducing 0.1M NaCl in nanofluid and water. Before the experiment, the system was allowed to attain steady-state open circuit potential (OCP) for 2 h. Variation of the corrosion with time was monitored from 2 to 216 h. Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was obtained at frequencies ranging from 100 kHz to 10 mHz, with an amplitude of 10 mV sinusoidal potential. Tafel polarization measurement was employed to quantify the corrosion rate of the sample by applying a sweep potential in the range 0.1 V below and above OCP at a scan rate of 1 mV/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimentation test rig for photo thermal conversion
ce:section.ce:para:
Direct absorption parabolic collector system used in the present study consist of a parabolic reflector/concentrator and a transparent glass-glass receiver. Anodized aluminium was used as the reflector in the parabola, whose shape and focal length is given by equations (4) and (5).
(4)
y = 0.925
x 2
(5)
f =
W 2
c o t θ +
W 2
16 f
where f is the focal length and, W and θ are the aperture width and rim angle.

The length and width of the parabola are 1.5 m and 1.05 m, respectively. The receiver is placed at the focal point of the reflector, which is 0.27 m from the vertex in the present case. The dimensions of the collector are presented in Table 1. The receiver tube used in the present study is a transparent glass-glass which is of quartz having high transmittance (0.91). The annulus of the receiver tube is evacuated partially to eliminate the losses due to convection. An automatic solar tracker was employed to track the sun so that direct solar radiation falls on the parabolic reflector throughout the day. Calibrated T-type thermocouples are employed to measure the fluid temperatures at the inlet and outlet. The required fluid flow rate was ensured using a flow meter and valve. The flow rate considered was in the range of 60 L/h to 90 L/h, with an interval of 10 L/h.

Fig. 3 (a) presents a schematic of the hydraulic cycle adopted in the present investigation, which is based on the ASHRE standard 93–2010, and Fig. 3(b) shows the fabricated parabolic collector system. The working fluid was pumped to the receiver from a reservoir. The photo-thermal conversion is achieved at the receiver when fluid passes through it. The fluid temperature increases as a function of flow rate and solar radiation. The hot working fluid is directed to the heat exchanger, where it is cooled to the initial temperature and send to the reservoir. The flow rate was varied from 60 to 90 L/h, and the inlet temperature was fixed at 30 °C.

A mathematical model was developed for the direct absorption parabolic collector by considering energy balance at the glass cover, absorber glass and heat transfer fluid, which is schematically represented in Fig. 4 . Accounting for the optical and thermal energy balance yields three nonlinear equations, which were solved using the Newton Raphson method. The experimental results were used to verify the mathematical model.

An energy balance that accounts for the total energy incident on the parabolic collector and the thermal energy finally gained by the heat transfer fluid, considering the reflectivity, transitivity and absorptivity at various components of the collector, is presented as follows.

Total energy balance equation is as follows (Eqn. (6)).
(6)
I
i n c i d e n t
=
(
I
a b d − m i r r o r
+
I
a b d − g l a s s
c o v e r
+

r e f − g l a s s

I

g l a s s
+
I
a b d − a b s o r b e r
g l a s s
+
I
a b d − h t f
)
.
L .
W

Where Iabd-mirror, Iabd-glass cover, Iabd-absorber glass, Iabd-htf are radiation absorbed by mirror, glass cover, absorber glass and heat transfer fluid. Iref-glass cover and Iref- absorber glass is the radiation reflected by glass cover and absorber glass respectively.

Radiation absorbed by the solar selective material convert it into thermal energy based on the absorptivity [27]. In the present study radiation absorbed by the nanofluid/heat transfer fluid (Iabd-htf) in equation (6) is given by Eqn. (7).

Energy balance at Glass cover: A significant portion of the solar radiation falling on the glass cover are transmitted through it. A small amount of the incident radiation is absorbed by the glass cover. Thermal losses occur from the outer surface of the glass cover in the form of convection and radiation heat transfer to the ambient. The thermal energy lost from the inner components in the form of convection and radiation heat transfer are absorbed on the inner surface of the glass cover. The mathematical representation is given in Eqn. (11). Qrad ag-g, Qconv ag-g are the radiative and convective heat transfer from absorber glass which is absorbed by the glass cover. Qrad g-amb, Qconv g-amb stands for the radiation and convection heat transfer from the glass cover to the atmosphere.
(11)

The Radiative and convective heat transfer from glass cover to the ambient is represented in Eqn. (15) and (16).

Energy balance at absorber glass: The solar radiation transmitted from the glass cover is directed to the heat transfer fluid with an intermediate absorption (
I
a b d − a g
) at absorber glass. A portion of the thermal energy gained by the heat transfer fluid could be transferred to the absorber glass through convection (Qconv ag-htf). In addition, there could be energy losses from the absorber glass by convection and radiation towards the glass cover. The energy balance at absorber glass is presented in Eqn. (17).

are given by Eqn. (13) and (14), respectively.

Energy balance at the heat transfer fluid: The solar radiation transmitted from absorber glass tube is absorbed by the heat transfer fluid (Iabd-htf). A portion of the energy gained by the heat transfer fluid is transferred to the absorber tube in the form of convection. Reflective and radiative losses from the nanofluid are neglected.

The experimental uncertainty of the data was estimated using the method described by Moffat [28]. The general form of uncertainty technique is presented in equation (27). The calculated uncertainty is presented in Table 2 .

Morphology of the synthesized C-dot was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 5 . It is evident that the synthesized particles exhibit a size less than 10 nm, and the particle size varies from 4 nm to 8 nm. The EDAX spectrum given in Fig. 6 confirms the presence of carbon particles. The major atomic percentage of the spectrum is contributed by carbon and oxygen (45.83% and 49.79%). The presence of oxygen is due to the existence of C–O and CO bonds. However, a small atomic percentage (4.38%) of Cu is evident in the EDAX spectrum, which is due to the reduction of copper acetate in the presence of ascorbic acid. The weight percentage of the copper is approximately 10%. The higher density of copper (8.96 g/cm3) as compared to carbon (1.9 g/cm3) resulted in the high weight percentage. However, the volume of copper will be much lower than the carbon. In addition, the atomic percentage of C, O and Cu are 45.83, 49.79 and 4.38%, respectively, from which it is clear that the amount of copper is less than 5% and hence could be neglected. The presence of C–O and CO bonds is further confirmed from the peaks at 1175 and 1638 wavenumbers in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 7 ). The absorption spectrum obtained from the UV–vis spectroscopy for the carbon dot particles is shown in Fig. 8 . The peak observed at 270 nm corresponds to the excitation exhibited by the carbon electron [29]. Fig. 9 presents the transmittance spectrum of carbon dot nanofluid at specified concentration, from which the absorptivity of nanofluid was analyzed. The solar weighted absorption fraction, which is the weighted average of total rays absorbed by the C-dot based nanofluid, was estimated from the transmittance spectrum of UV–vis NIR spectroscopy. In the present study, the temperature-dependent viscosity of the heat transfer fluid was estimated using a Brookfield rheometer. The value of viscosity observed at various temperatures is presented in Table 3 . As can be seen from the table, the viscosity of the nanofluid noted at 25 °C is 0.99 Cp, whereas at 65 °C it is 0.57 Cp. It is clear from the viscosity values that the influence of C-dot particles is not much significant in flow resistance.

Solar absorptivity of the nanofluid was quantified by employing a UV–vis–NIR spectroscopy. The solar weighted absorption fraction was arrived using the equation Depth of penetration is the depth at which the solar radiation travels and is an important parameter that determines the performance of a collector. Since the diameter of the absorber used for the present study is 1.5 cm, the total incident radiation has to be arrested within this depth. This is possible only at significantly high values of SWAF. Since the C-dot nanofluid obtained after the synthesis was found to be having low absorptivity, the concentration of C-dot in the nanofluid was increased by vaporizing excess water from the supernatant. To achieve this the supernatant was boiled. The decrement in the volume of the fluid and corresponding SWAF was noted at various stages of boiling, viz 0.7, 0.6, 0.5 and 0.4 times that of the initial volume. A broad absorption spectrum in the visible region was noted for the volume of nanofluid after boiling, corresponding to 0.4 times the initial volume, and the corresponding SWAF obtained is 0.92. Fig. 10 shows the depth of penetration of the optimized C-dot nanofluid. It is apparent that the nanofluid absorbs nearly 94% at the penetration depth of 1.5 cm. The performance of optimized C-dot nanofluid is then investigated in a parabolic collector. In the present study, nanofluid is synthesized using the one-step method, in which the nanofluid is obtained in the form of supernatant after centrifugation. Due to this reason, the particle cannot be separated from the base fluid directly. Hence the concentration of the nanofluid could not be estimated directly.

The colloidal stability of nanofluids is a prime metric that ensures its practical application and consistency in performance. Zeta potential analysis is one of the widely adopted techniques for stability estimation. However, for samples with high optical absorptivity, this technique is not suitable due to the fact that the light source finds it difficult to transmit through the fluid. Hence, in the present study, the stability of the nanofluid was analyzed using the centrifugation method. The stability analysis was performed on the final optimized C-Dot nanofluid by centrifuging it for 30 min at 12,000 rpm. The nanofluid samples were analyzed using UV–vis–NIR spectroscopy before and after the centrifugation. The variation in the transmittance of nanofluid before and after the centrifugation was estimated. Fig. 11 presents the transmittance spectrum before and after centrifugation. It is evident that the SWAF estimated before and after the centrifugation is nearly the same, which indicates that the synthesized C-dot nanofluid is stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion analysis of C-dot nanofluid on mild steel
ce:section.ce:para:
The corrosion behaviour of C-dot nanofluid was investigated using the electrochemical workstation. The potential was induced after ensuring a stable OCP achieved after 2 h. The stable OCP noted for nanofluid was −0.38 V, which, when compared to water (OCP: −0.66 V) is more cathodic in nature, indicating lesser electrochemical activity for mild steel exposed to C-dot nanofluid. The fitted Nyquist plot from EIS spectra of C-dot nanofluid at various time intervals and DI water after 2 h are shown in Fig. 12 . Higher the semi-circle radius of the Nyquist plot, higher will be the corrosion resistance. It is seen that after 216 h of immersion, the charge transfer resistance of C-dot nanofluid reduced to that corresponding to DI water after 2 h. This indicates that the C-dot nanofluid will create corrosion similar to that created by DI water in 2 h, only after an immersion duration of 216 h. The corrosion current and potential were estimated by extrapolating the Tafel plot (Tafel method), as shown in Fig. 13 . The positive shift in Tafel curve for C-dot nanofluid shows the more protective nature of mild steel exposed to C-dot nanofluid, as compared to DI water. The corrosion current for DI water and C-dot nanofluid was found to be 3.2
×
10−5 A and 8.1
×
10−6 A, respectively. Lower corrosion current was observed for C-dot nanofluid as compared to DI water. The corrosion current is directly proportional to corrosion rate. The corrosion rate of mild steel in C-dot nanofluid was estimated as 0.094 mm/year, while the corresponding value for water was 0.372 mm/year. Hence, it is evident that the mild steel exposed to C-dot nanofluid has lower corrosion rate than mild steel exposed to DI water.

The water in presence of O2 when exposed to mild steel, being the working electrode, actively participate in the electrochemical reaction by oxidising Fe to Fe2+ and forming various oxide of iron. The oxide film formed on the iron surface is a temporary protection from further corrosion. These oxide layers, being highly porous, allows the oxygen into it and thus exposing base iron to oxygen resulting in further corrosion. The exposure of C-dot nanofluid with mild steel reduces the chance of water molecules getting in contact with the working electrode. Fig. 14 schematically represent the interactions when mild steel is exposed to water and nanofluid. In Fig. 14(a), it could be clearly observed that the working electrode being enveloped by water increasing the chance of corrosion. From Fig. 14(b), it is evident that the working electrode is covered by water and C-Dot particles, which reduces the exposure of working electrode to water resulting in a lesser chance for the formation of the oxide layer and thus a reduction in corrosion rate is experienced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental performance evaluation of parabolic collector
ce:section.ce:para: The experimentation on direct absorption solar collector was conducted under a clear sky condition during the month of March at the National Institute of Technology Calicut, India. The experiments were conducted using the optimized C-Dot/water nanofluid to analyze and compare its performance in a direct absorption solar collector with that of the base fluid. The solar radiation during the experimentation was in the range of 650–980 W/m2. The outlet temperate of the nanofluid was measured at intervals of 5 min and averaged every 30 min, from 10:00 to 16:00. The flow rates of heat transfer fluid were varied from 60 L/h to 90 L/h, which corresponds to Reynolds number in the range 1968–2952. The outlet temperature of the heat transfer fluid decreases with the increase in the flow rate, as shown in Fig. 15 . As can be seen from Fig. 15(a–d), the outlet temperature increases from 10:00 in the morning while the radiation is 720 W/m2 and reaches a maximum at the highest radiation of 980 W/m2. The maximum outlet temperature of 43.49 °C was noticed at 60 L/h and the minimum of 39.4 °C at 90 L/h for C-Dot nanofluid at the maximum radiation. The corresponding outlet temperatures for water were 33.33 °C and 32.08 °C at 60 L/h and 90 L/h. It is evident that the performance of water is 30.48 and 22.81% inferior to that of C-Dot nanofluid at 60 and 90 L/h. The maximum and minimum temperatures observed at 90 L/h for C-dot nanofluid was 39.4 °C and 36.67 °C, respectively. The improved solar absorptivity of the C-dot has contributed highly to enhance the outlet temperature of heat transfer fluid. In a conventional surface absorption solar collector, a shift in heat gain by the fluid could be observed during the transition from laminar to turbulent flow due to the intermixing of the fluid created by the eddies. However, in a direct absorption solar collector, the influence of convective heat transfer in transferring the solar energy to the fluid is minimal since the solar radiations are directly absorbed by the nanofluid itself. The thermal efficiency of the collector estimated using the Eqn. (18)–(20) was found to increase with the flow rate, as shown in Fig. 16 (a–d). The maximum and minimum thermal efficiency observed for C-dot nanofluid were 73.41% and 44.67% at 90 L/h, which translates to the enhancement of 57.62% and 34.85% as compared to water. At a flow rate of 60 L/h and solar radiations of 650 and 980 W/m2, the maximum and minimum thermal efficiencies are 43.04% and 70.21% for C-dot nanofluid, while that for water is 7.33% and 13.20%, respectively. The comparison of the efficiency of nanofluid and base fluid at 90 L/h is presented in Table 4 . The enhanced heat transfer capability of nanofluids is primarily attributed to the Brownian motion of nanoparticles, nanoparticle clustering and micro convection. The presence of particles of size less than 10 nm in the C-dot nanofluids will create localized microturbulence and Brownian motion, which promotes intermixing of the fluid and enhances heat transfer performance.

The variation in thermal efficiency with solar radiation at different times during the day for various flow rates are shown in Fig. 16(a–d). The maximum thermal efficiency was obtained during 12:00 noon to 1:00 p.m., corresponding to the occurrence of peak solar radiation.

Fig. 17 shows the variation of average entropy generation with flow rate for C-dot nanofluid and base fluid. The entropy generation occurs during the conversion of solar energy to thermal energy (E1) and due to the thermal losses to the ambient (E2). The maximum E1 of 2.505 W/K at 60 L/h, the minimum was 2.619 W/K at 90 L/h. However, a slight increase in the total entropy (E1+E2) was noticed at a higher Reynolds number. As the flow rate increases the flow pattern shift towards the turbulent region, due to which there exist a large temperature gradient between absorber glass and the outlet fluid temperature. Subsequently, the fluid friction and heat transfer irreversibility result in a reduced availability of converted thermal energy. The individual entropy components, E1 and E2, shows a different trend with the flow rate. The entropy generation during the conversion of solar energy to thermal energy (E1) is the dominant contributor to the total generated entropy in the case of C-dot nanofluid. This is due to the fact that in nanofluid, the temperature gain is high compared to base fluid and hence the contribution of E1 is high, as given by Eqn. (21). Due to the increased absorptivity of nanofluid the optical losses associated with it is less and the consequent temperature increase results lower E2 for nanofluid based collector when compared to water. For water, the solar thermal conversion being less, the associated entropy generation (E1) is also less. But the energy loss in the case of water is high since it works mainly on the surface absorption mode, and hence the associated entropy generation (E2) was found to be high.

The exergy destruction of the collector was estimated using Eqn. (23), and its variation with flow and time are presented in Fig. 18 . The dependency of theoretical and experimental exergy destruction on solar radiation is illustrated in the figures. As can be seen from Fig. 18 a-d, at higher solar irradiance, the exergy destruction increases with the flow rate. The maximum exergy destruction of 956 W was observed at a flow rate of 90 L/h, whereas the corresponding minimum value is 639W. The maximum and minimum exergy destruction observed at 60 L/h is 951 W and 636W, respectively. As flow rate increases, the thermal efficiency of the collector increases with increased entropy generation as a result, the exergy destruction also increases. The maximum energy conversion and transfer is achieved at the highest flow rate of the heat transfer fluid. To be specific, the amount of thermal energy gained by the heat transfer fluid through solar thermal conversion is found to be enhanced at higher flow rates. However, the effective conversion and transfer of energy are achieved at a lower flow rate so as to make it the available form. This might be due to the transfer of flow pattern from laminar to turbulent. A careful observation of Eq. (21) and Fig. 18 shows that with an increase in flow rate, the corresponding logarithmic decrement in the temperature difference is less and consequently, the entropy generation increases. Since a considerable increase in the exergy loss is occurring during the energy conversion, the change in the flow rate influences exergy destruction significantly. Considering the solar radiation as an input parameter in the variation of exergy destruction was found to be increased with the solar radiation. Maximum destruction of 956W was noted at the radiation of 980W/m2, and a minimum of 706W was observed at a lower heat flux of 720W/m2 at 90 L/h.

The experimental data noted was compared with that of the mathematical model developed, and the variation in the data are presented in Table 5 . The maximum and minimum variation in the fluid outlet temperature for experimental and mathematical analysis were 1.49 °C and 0.42 °C, respectively. The maximum variation noted in the thermal efficiency was 7.75%, and the minimum variation noted was 3.28%. The average deviation in thermal efficiency was obtained as 5.73%. The maximum and minimum variation of 3.21 W and 0.85 W, respectively, were noted for exergy destruction. The collector efficiency obtained in the present study has been compared with the results of a previously published article in which SiO2/Ag–CuO nanofluid was used [3]. The maximum solar absorptivity observed in the present study is 92%, whereas in the case of SiO2/Ag–CuO nanofluid it is 82%. The corresponding collector efficiencies at 90 L/h is 73.14% and 64.13%, respectively.

Table 6 presents the cost involved in the preparation of C-Dot nanofluid. The estimation includes the cost of materials used and energy (electricity) required for the synthesis. The estimated cost is then weighted with respect to the absorptivity and thermal efficiency of the fluids used (nanofluid and water). As per this comparison, the calculated cost per unit absorptivity for DI water was $ 1.5, while that for C-dot nanofluid is $ 0.9824. Moreover, the cost per efficiency of the nanofluid and water was estimated to analyze its commercial suitability in various direct absorption solar collectors. The estimated cost per thermal efficiency of the C-dot nanofluid based collector is $ 0.0123, whereas it is $ 0.0171 for DI water-based collector.

This article illustrates the synthesis, characterization, and influence of C-dot nanofluid on direct absorption parabolic solar collector. The corrosion analysis and cost estimation were performed to widen the scope of the synthesized nanofluid in various industrial applications. The nanofluid was synthesized using a simple one-pot synthesis technique. Solar absorptivity for the synthesized C-Dot nanofluid, without losing its colloidal stability, was 92%. The observed maximum collector efficiency was 73.41%, which is 57.62% higher than the base fluid. In addition, a maximum entropy generation of 3.03 W/K and exergy destruction of 922.3 W was observed for the C-dot nanofluid at 90 L/h. The presence of carbon dot in the base fluid also exhibited high resistance to corrosion, and the quantified corrosion rate was 0.094 mm/year, which is 25.27% lower than that for DI water. The estimated cost per efficiency for C-dot nanofluid is lower than that of the base fluid, which enhances its potential to be a commercial product. Considering the enhanced collector efficiency, low corrosivity and low production cost analysis, the presented nanofluid could gain wide attention as a prospective working fluid for solar thermal systems.
Albin Joseph: Conceived and designed the analysis, Collected the data, contributed data/ analysis tool, Performed the analysis, Wrote the paper.
Shijo Thomas: Conceived and designed the analysis, contributed data/ analysis tool, Performed the analysis, Wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
